coredata.dc:title: Crude fiber and protein rich cottonseed meal derived carbon quantum dots composite porous carbon for supercapacitor
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tao
Peng, Lina
Wu, Dongling
Chen, Bolang
Jia, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Cottonseed meal (CM) contains abundant crude fiber and crude protein, which is a kind of high quality carbon precursor. Herein, CM is hydrothermal treated by H2SO4 solution to get the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and hydrothermal pre-carbonization CM (HCM). The CQDs and HCM composite are carbonize-activated with K2CO3 and obtain the N, O, S self-doping porous carbon. CQDs with good crystallinity and abundant heteroatoms attach themselves to HCM, that increase the conductivity and heteroatoms contents of the obtained porous carbon. The prepared porous carbon possesses good conductivity, reasonable pore size distribution, large specific surface area and rich heteroatomic doping, which result in an ultrahigh specific capacitance of 477 F g−1 in 6 M KOH. Moreover, the assembled symmetric capacitor using a novel K2CO3-glycol-arginine deep eutectic solvent electrolyte displays a high voltage window of 2.0 V, superior energy density of 45.6 Wh kg−1 and a wide range of working temperatures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crude fiber and protein
Carbon quantum dots
Porous carbon
Supercapacitor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22269022 ), and Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (No. 2022D01C24 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for synthesizing of HSDCM-3.
The fluorescent emission spectra in different excitation wavelengths (a), the optical image under ultraviolet light (inset), (b) FT-IR and (c) TEM image of CQDs and the fourier transform image (d).
SEM images of (a) HSDCM-2, (b) HSDCM-3, (c) HSDCM-4 and (d) Mapping image of HSDCM-3, (e) TEM images and (f) the electron diffraction pattern (inset) of HSDCM-3.
(a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of all samples; (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (d) the pore size distribution of HSDCM-3.
(a) XPS survey spectra, (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) N 1 s, (e) S 2p and (f) C, N, S and O contents and the N groups ratio of HSDCM-3.

Capacitive performances tested in a three-electrode system in 6 M KOH. (a) CV curves at 50 mV s−1, (b) GCD curves at 0.5 A g−1, (c) CV and (d) GCD curves of HSDCM-3, (e) capacitance calculated at different current densities, (f) the EDLC and pseudo contribution tested at 1 mV s−1, the pseudocapacitance and accurate EDLC contribution of HSDCM-3, (g) EIS curves, (h) the phase angle with the frequency and (i) the Z-ω−1/2 plots.

Capacitive performance of HSDCM-3 in a two-electrode system in DES electrolyte: (a) CV curves tested at different scan rates and (b) GCD curves tested at different current densities, (c) Ragone plot at different working temperatures, (d) the cycling stability.

head.ce:title: Crude fiber and protein rich cottonseed meal derived carbon quantum dots composite porous carbon for supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, Xinjiang, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources, College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemistry and Utilization of Carbon Based Energy Resources; College of Chemistry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi, 830046, Xinjiang, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics and Chemistry Analysis Center, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Physics and Chemistry Analysis Center, Xinjiang University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Physics and Chemistry Analysis Center, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cottonseed meal (CM) contains abundant crude fiber and crude protein, which is a kind of high quality carbon precursor. Herein, CM is hydrothermal treated by H2SO4 solution to get the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and hydrothermal pre-carbonization CM (HCM). The CQDs and HCM composite are carbonize-activated with K2CO3 and obtain the N, O, S self-doping porous carbon. CQDs with good crystallinity and abundant heteroatoms attach themselves to HCM, that increase the conductivity and heteroatoms contents of the obtained porous carbon. The prepared porous carbon possesses good conductivity, reasonable pore size distribution, large specific surface area and rich heteroatomic doping, which result in an ultrahigh specific capacitance of 477 F g−1 in 6 M KOH. Moreover, the assembled symmetric capacitor using a novel K2CO3-glycol-arginine deep eutectic solvent electrolyte displays a high voltage window of 2.0 V, superior energy density of 45.6 Wh kg−1 and a wide range of working temperatures.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs composited porous carbon was prepared by hydrothermal pre-carbonization of CM followed K2CO3 activation process. And the DES-based capacitor shows a wide working temperature and superior energy density.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: I have shared the link to my data at the Attach File step.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass resources commonly included plants and animal matter, which were produced by human and animal life activities. The earth produced about 140 million tons of biomass resources, most of which were consumed by humans [1]. However, there were still numerous waste biomass abandoned directly or handed rudely, which produced a lot of unfriendly pollution to the environment. How to convert biomass especially the abandoned biomass resources into functional materials was an important issue, and it had aroused the wide spread interest [2].

With the development of economy and society, energy and environmental issues were increasingly prominent, which promoted the research of new energy storage and conversion devices. Supercapacitors attracted so much attention for its long cycle life, high power density and fast charging-discharging ability [3,4]. It is well known that electrode material was one of the important influencing factors for the performance of supercapacitor [5]. Biomass had been widely explored and used as electrode material in supercapacitor due to its rich resources, low cost and renewable characteristics. And biomass-based porous carbon was studied deeply and considered as one kind of potential electrode material for its rich porous structure, chemical stability, good conductivity and abundant heteroatoms composition [6]. Another important factor was the electrolyte, its potential window directly determined the energy density of supercapacitor, so it was of great significance to explore electrolyte with a large potential window [7,8].

The normal method of synthesizing biomass-based porous carbon included activation method, hydrothermal carbonization, template method and molten salt carbonization [9]. Among them, hydrothermal treatment could play a role of pre-activation and pore-forming agent, and it also could increase the quality of O-based groups of the hydrochar, which was beneficial to improve the distribution of the pores and increase the O doping [10]. Chemical activation could produce a large number of micropores or mesoporous structures, which provided an amount of active sites for forming electronic double layer capacitance [11]. Hence, hydrothermal carbonization combined with chemical activation was an efficient method in preparing biomass-based porous carbon.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were a fascinating member of carbon family, they were quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles, the core was composed of sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms, and its surface contained active functional group sites [12,13]. It could be used as an electronic transport medium or bridge to improve the conductivity of CQDs composites [14–18]. Biomass was also a potential precursor for the preparation of CQDs because of its rich heteroatoms and high carbon content. Nguyen et al. prepared banana peels-derived CQDs and porous carbon material at the same time via the hydrothermal reaction, and the prepared porous carbon electrode possessed a specific capacitance of 199 F g−1 [19]. Bu et al. made N-doped graphene quantum dots growing evenly on the surface of polyacrylic acid, and the obtained carbon electrode had a remarkable specific capacitance of 419 F g−1 [20]. Oskueyan et al. prepared CQDs from carrot juice by hydrothermal method, and the synthesized composites via in-situ polymerization material displayed a high specific capacitance of 396 F g−1 [21]. The application of biomass-based CQDs in the modification of electrode materials for supercapacitors was a significant work.

CM was obtained after pressing cottonseed for oil, as one of the by-products of cotton, it was rich in crude protein, crude fiber and other organic matter. The rich in protein made it suitable for use as protein feed, but the toxic gossypol contented in CM would harm the livestock, which vastly limited the additive amount of CM in animal feed. It was of great significance to explore the new use of CM and promote its efficient utilization. So far, studying on preparing carbon materials around various parts of cotton, such as cotton stalks, cotton threads, cotton seed shells and cotton leaves, and applying them to supercapacitors have been reported. For example, Jiang et al. prepared a cotton silk based porous carbon material, it exhibited a high specific surface area (SSA) of 1508 m2 g−1, hierarchical porous structure and a specific capacitance of 278 F g−1 in 6 M KOH [22]. Tian et al. used cotton stalks as precursor to prepare the high SSA porous carbon, and the assembled solid symmetric supercapacitor possessed an energy density of 12.5 Wh kg−1 [23]. Mao et al. prepared a hierarchical porous carbon from cotton leaves, the specific capacitance of the obtained carbon was 212 F g−1, and a symmetric supercapacitor was assembled using the natural seawater electrolyte, it delivered an energy density of 7.36 Wh kg−1 [24]. Jiang et al. used cottonseed hull as carbon precursor to prepare high SSA (1694 m2 g−1) and excellent specific capacitance (283 F g−1) porous carbon [25]. Recently, we reported a simple method that using hydrothermal treatment and K2CO3 activation process to prepare CM-based porous carbon, it displayed a high specific capacitance of 340 F g−1 [26], the residual rate of hydrochar was relatively low (70%), and the filtrate (cloudy liquid) after hydrothermal treatment should possess many carbohydrates that decomposed from CM. Hence，whether the waste filtrate could be used to improve the specific capacitance of the CM-based porous carbon material. What’s more, the potential window of our previously reported K2CO3-glycol deep eutectic solvent (DES) electrolyte was only 1.8 V [26], it was necessary to further enlarge the potential window and thus improve the energy density of the assembled supercapacitor.

On the basis of the above results, in this study, H2SO4 was used to pre-carbonize the CM in the hydrothermal carbonization process and composited the CQDs from the filtrate of the hydrothermal reaction with the hydrothermal treatment CM solid. After the carbonization and activation with K2CO3, finally got the N, O, S self-doping porous carbon with the enhanced conductivity, rich heteroatoms doping contents (N：1.43 at%, O：13.70 at%, S：0.21 at%) and a high surface area of 1966 m2 g−1. As the capacitor electrode material, it exhibited a superior capacitance of 477 F g−1 in 6 M KOH. What’s more, we prepared a large potential window (2.0 V) of DES electrolyte based on the addition of amino acid into our previously reported K2CO3-glycol DES electrolyte. The assembled symmetrical supercapacitor with the prepared porous carbon and DES electrolyte exhibited a remarkable energy density of 45.6 Wh kg−1, and the device could work properly at extreme temperatures (−25 to 105 ℃).

Firstly, the dried CM was crushed in a grinder and then passed through a 140-mesh screen and got the yellow powder. Then 5 g CM, 47 mL of deionized water and 3 mL of H2SO4 were mixed and placed in a 100 mL hydrothermal reactor, kept it at 180 ℃ for 12 h in oven. The solid-liquid mixture was added with K2CO3 to neutralize H2SO4, then the mixture was dialyzed, after freeze-dried, washed with deionized water and dried to get dark brown powder (HSDCM). Then, mixed 0.9 g K2CO3 and 0.3 g HSDCM (mass ratio: 3:1) and carbonized at 800 ℃ for 2 h under N2 flow at a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1. Finally, the product was cooled naturally to the room temperature, and washed using 1 M HCl and distilled water until the filtrate was neutral. The obtained black powder was dried in an oven at 80 ℃ for 24 h and denoted as HSDCM-3. To study the effect of K2CO3 ratio, a series of samples were prepared as the same process of HSDCM-3 and named as HSDCM-2 and HSDCM-4, where 2 and 4 meant the mass ratio of K2CO3 to HSDCM. To investigate the effect of CQDs, the hydrothermal treated CM mixture was filtered and carbonized the solid following the above mentioned process and obtained HSCM-3.

Electrode preparation and testing are as follows: the prepared series samples, PTFE solution and acetylene black with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 were mixed uniform, then daubed it on the Ni foam and dried it at 80 ℃ for 12 h. As for the two-electrode system, the two prepared matching electrodes were used to assemble a symmetrical capacitor (CR2016 stainless steel coin cell) with glass fiber as membrane. The CHI 760E was used to test the electrochemical performance.

Electrochemical measurements: The specific capacitance is calculated by Eq. (1).
(1) C = I∆t/(m∆V)
Where, ∆t is the discharge time, I is the current density, m is the weight of active material and ∆V is the voltage window.

The EDLC contribution and pseudocapacitance contribution are distinguished by Eqs. (2) and (3).
(2) i = k1v + k2v1/2
(3) i/v1/2 = k1v1/2 + k2
Where the k2v1/2 and k1v represent the diffusion-dependent partand surface dependent part, and the i represents the current density.

The ion diffusion constant is calculated from the plots in the low-frequency region by Eqs. (4), (5) and (6).
(4) Zre = Rct + Rs + δω−1/2
(5) ω = 2πf
(6) D = R2T2 / (2A2n4F4C2δ2)

Where D is the ion diffusion constant, R is the gas coefficient, T is the absolute temperature, A is the surface area, F is the Faraday’s constant, C is the concentration, n is the number of electrons per molecule oxidized, δ is the Warburg factor, and ω is the angle frequency.

The specific capacitance and energy density of the assembled device are calculated by the following Eqs. (7) and (8).
(7) Cs = I∆t/(m∆V)
(8) E = CsV2/(2*3·6)
The DES electrolyte preparation, Characterization, and other details were shown in supporting information.

The preparation process of HSDCM-3 was shown in Fig. 1. In the hydrothermal process, a part component of CM was decomposed and then some macropore formed (Fig. S1), the HSCM displayed a rough surface and rich porous structure, which proved the addition of H2SO4 in the hydrothermal process could increase the degree of pre-carbonization of biomass [27,28], and it was also beneficial to form the porosity in carbon precursor. The micromolecule matter from the decomposed cellulose, protein and other component underwent the polymerization reaction via intermolecular/intramolecular condensation of hydroxy aldehydes, dehydration condensation and aromatization, and finally formed CQDs [29,30]. The CQDs bonded to the surface and the channel of the hydrothermal treated HSCM, which could increase the degree of graphitization, after the K2CO3 activation, the high SSA and good conductivity of porous carbon were obtained.

Fig. 2a showed the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs. With the increasing of excitation wavelength from 330 to 390 nm, the emission wavelength did not shift significantly in 450 nm, but the emission intensity first increased and then decreased, and the intensity of CQDs was the strongest at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which meant 360 nm was the maximum excitation wavelength. And the Fig. 2a (inset) showed the CQDs solution emitted bright blue-green light under UV irradiation, which was consistent with the analysis result of the fluorescence emission spectrum. FT-IR analysis of the CQDs solution (Fig. 2b) showed that the peaks at 3450, 2074, 1636 and 1301 cm−1, corresponding to the vibration of -NH2/-OH, -CN, CO and CC, respectively [31], indicating that the solution contained a large number of functional groups. The existence of CC functional group proved that the carbon point had a certain degree of graphitization, which provided conditions for improving the electrical conductivity of CQDs. Abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups in CQDs could increase the heteroatoms doping content of CQDs composites. Fig. 2c showed that the CQDs were solid quasi-spherical particles with a diameter of about 2.0 nm. In the hydrothermal process, some components of CM were first hydrolyzed into intermediates (such as glucose and amino acids) with the catalysis of acid, and then the glucose was further dehydrated and polycondensation to form intermediates such as 5-hydroxymethylfurfural. The formed products underwent intermolecular/intramolecular hydroxyl aldehyde condensation, dehydration condensation, aromatization and resulted the oligomers. As the reaction continued, when the oligomer concentration increased to the critical saturation concentration, the polymerization reaction accelerated and a large number of carbon nuclei were generated. Small molecules and oligomers grew around the carbon nuclei to form the relatively large spherical carbon [32], that was the CQDs. The CQDs were adsorbed onto the surface or the nearby channels of HSCM to reduce the surface activation energy of CQDs, the formation and optimization of the conductive network could improve the graphitization degree of HSCM. The Fourier transform image in Fig. 2d showed bright spots, which proved once again that the CQDs had a certain graphitization degree, and it could be used as electron conduction medium or bridge to improve the conductivity of HSDCM.

The morphology of HSDCM-X was shown in Fig. 3 and SEM images of different H2SO4 addition amounts were shown in Fig. S2. All samples showed three-dimensional porous structures. HSDCM-2 showed relatively large sheet morphology, and had fewer pores (Fig. 3a). As the ratio of K2CO3 activator increased, it could be observed that the pore size decreased, the rich pore formed and increased the 3D interconnected porous structures in HSDCM-3 (Fig. 3b). And further increased the K2CO3 content, the sheets were broken, pores were collapsed and the larger pores were obviously generated due to over-activation of K2CO3 in HSDCM-4 (Fig. 3c), it indicating that the ratio of K2CO3 was the main factor that determined the morphology and pore structure of samples, the uniform porosity and suitable 3D interconnected pore structure of the sample prepared by HSDCM required an appropriate amount of K2CO3. Fig. 3d showed the element Mapping of HSDCM-3, the C, N, S and O elements were evenly distributed in HSDCM-3. On the one hand, the doping of heteroatoms was conducive to enhance the hydrophilicity and wettability of the electrode surface, which could promote rapid diffusion of ions. On the other hand, it could provide pseudocapacitance and improve the capacitance performance of carbon electrode. Thus, it could improve the capacitive performance of the prepared porous carbon. To further analyze the microstructure of sample, TEM analysis was carried out and shown in Fig. 3e. It could be observed that HSDCM-3 had an obvious 3D porous structure and abundant meso-macroporous structure. The absence of significant lattice fringes in Fig. 3f suggested that HSDCM-3 was predominantly amorphous carbon, which might be due to the relatively low carbonation temperature and the activation effect of K2CO3. The bright diffraction patterns in the electron diffraction image in Fig. 3f corresponded to the crystal planes (002) and (100) of carbon respectively [33], which proved that it had a certain degree of graphitization. The high conductivity could reduce the impedance, enhance the electrochemical performance and was also conducive to the increase of the effective specific active surface area of HSDCM-3. Thus, the capacitance performance of HSDCM-3 could be improved.

Fig. 4a showed the XRD patterns of HSDCM-X. In all samples, the typical diffraction peaks occurred around 22° and 44°, corresponding to (002) crystal planes of amorphous carbon and (100) crystal planes of graphite phase, respectively [34]. With increasing K2CO3 ratio, the peak intensity of (002) crystal plane of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3 and HSDCM-4 was very weak, which might be caused by the scattering of X-ray on the surface of the obtained material due to a large number of micromoles, leading to the enhancement of background signal [35]. In addition, the appearance of (100) crystal plane diffraction peak indicated that the material had a certain degree of graphitized structure. Fig. 4b showed the Raman spectra, the D peak at 1350 cm−1 was induced form disordered carbon, the G peak at 1580 cm−1 represented the graphitization characteristics of carbon. The wide D peak is fitted into D1 peak (impurity generation), D2 peak (caused by the vibration of sp3 carbon atoms) and D3 peak (formed by amorphous carbon) [36] at 1200, 1350 and 1490 cm−1, respectively. The area ratio of the fitted D2 peak to G peak AD2/AG reflected the disorder degree of carbon, the larger of the value meant the higher disorder degree of carbon [17]. The AD2/AG values of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3, HSDCM-4 and HSCM-3 were 1.70, 1.71, 1.72 and 1.84, respectively (Fig. 4b). The results denoted that with the increase of the dosage of K2CO3, the AD2/AG value of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3 and HSDCM-4 had little difference, which meant the disorder of these three samples was similar. However, the AD2/AG value of HSCM-3 that without CQDs composite was apparently higher than HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3 and HSDCM-4, further indicating that the introduction of CQDs could improve the degree of graphitization. The specific surface area (SSA) and pore structure of HSCM-3 (without CQDs), HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3 and HSDCM-4 were analyzed by N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms. The SSA of HSCM-3, HSDCM-2 and HSDCM-4 were 1573, 1807 and 1489 m2 g−1, which were lower than that of HSDCM-3 (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 4c, it had the characteristics of both type Ⅰ and type Ⅳ hybrid isotherms [37]. In the low pressure area (P/P0<0.1), a sharply adsorption curve proved the existence of microporous structure; In the P/P0> 0.4, a typical hysteresis ring indicated the presence of mesopores, and when P/P0 approached 1, nitrogen adsorption increased, indicating the existence of macroporous structure. DFT analysis results showed that the SSA of HSDCM-3 was 1996 m2 g−1, and the pore volume was 0.839 cm3 g−1 [38]. The pore size distribution (calculated using DFT method) from micropore to macropore was shown in Fig. 4d. The pore size was mainly distributed at 1.2 nm (micropore), but also existence of mesopore and macropore. Based on the above mentioned results and combined with the SEM and TEM analysis, HSDCM-3 possessed a hierarchical porous structure, micropore could provide rich adsorption sites for electronic double layer capacitance, mesopore was beneficial to promote electrolyte ion diffusion, and macropore could be used as electrolyte storage area. In summary, HSDCM-3 had high SSA and reasonable pore size distribution, which was conducive to improve its capacitance performance.

To further analyze the surface chemical composition of HSDCM-3, XPS characterization was carried out and shown in Fig. 5. The binding energy at 285.1, 400.1, 532.6, and 178.7 eV corresponded to characteristic peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s, and S 2p, indicating heteroatoms were successfully doped into carbon (Fig. 5a). The characteristic peaks of C 1 s were fitted to four peaks at 289.0, 286.5, 284.7 and 284.1 eV (Fig. 5b) correspond to CO, C-N/C-O, C-C and CC components, respectively [39]. The CC functional group content was significantly in dominant position, indicating that the graphitization degree of HSDCM-3 was significantly enhanced, it was beneficial to the improvement of the electrical conductivity because of the modification CQDs. N 1 s was fitted into four peaks at 398.3, 400.1, 401.7 and 404.0 eV (Fig. 5c), representing N-5 (pyridine N, 41.3 at%), N-6 (pyrrole N, 11.9 at%), N-Q (graphite N, 34.2 at%) and N-O (nitrogen oxide, 12.6 at%) (Fig. 5f) [40]. The main nitrogen doping components were pyridine nitrogen and graphite nitrogen. Pyridine N was known to improve the surface wettability of the electrode, pyrrole N provided additional reversible pseudocapacitance, and graphite N and nitrogen oxide groups could provide positive charges, alter the charge densities, thus reduced the electron transfer resistance and increased the conductivity of carbon materials. Compared with our previous work [41–43] and HSCM-3, after modification by CQDs, the content of graphite nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen functional groups of the material were significantly increased, which could not only increase the conductivity of the material but also generate additional pseudocapacitance, thus improved the capacitance performance of the material. O 1 s was fitted to CO (531.3 eV) and C-O (532.9 eV) respectively (Fig. 5d) [44]. The CO species could offer negative charges to change the charge density of carbon, thus provided additional pseudocapacitance. The C-O group could improve the wettability of carbon electrode, which was conducive to improve the capacitive properties of carbon. High resolution XPS spectra of S 2p (Fig. 5e) showed the presence of C-S-C (168.8 eV and 169.8 eV) and C-SOx-C (164.5 eV and 165.6 eV) functional groups. Doping of S in the carbon skeleton could alter the electron cloud density of C to produce more defects, resulting in additional pseudocapacitance. The C-SOx-C group was beneficial to enhance the wettability of carbon and contributed to the improvement of capacitance performance [45]. As shown in Fig. 5f, the content of C, N, O and S elements in HSDCM-3 was 84.66, 1.43, 13.70 and 0.21 at% respectively, and the content of N and O was increased compared with that of HSCM-3 (Fig. S4), which was attributed to the modification of CQDs, the above FT-IR result revealed the CQDs contained N and O functional groups, hence increased the doping amount of N and O in HSDCM-3.

Firstly, the capacitance performance of serial HSDCM-X was tested in 6 M KOH in three-electrode system. The CVs and GCDs of samples prepared with different amounts of H2SO4 were shown in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. 6a, CV curves of a series of samples were irregular rectangles, indicating that the material mainly embodied the characteristics of electronic double layer capacitance. The GCD curves were shown in Fig. 6b, the discharge time of HSDCM-3 was the longest compared within all samples, indicating that it had the maximum specific capacitance. The specific capacitance of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3 and HSDCM-4 and HSCM-3 were 363, 392, 324 and 360 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1, respectively. Fig. 6c exhibited the CV curves of HSDCM-3 at different scanning rates, there was no obvious deformation, indicating that EDLC predominated in this sample. Fig. 6d showed the GCD curves of HSDCM-3 at different current densities (0.1–4 A g−1). When the current density was 0.1 A g−1, the specific capacitance could reach up to 477 F g−1. The specific capacitance under different current densities was shown in Fig. 6e, when the current density was 100 A g−1, the capacitance of HSDCM-3 could still reach 220 F g−1 (higher than 161 F g−1 of HSCM-3).

As shown in Fig. 6f, the capacitance contribution of the EDLC was 85.2% calculated by Eqs. 2 and 3 at 1 mV s−1, which was due to its high SSA and a large number of micropores, it could provide a large number of active sites to form the electronic double layer. When the scanning rate increased from 1 mV s−1 to 7 mV s−1, the contribution value of pseudocapacitance decreased from 14.8% to 2.6%, indicating that the EDLC contribution increased with the increase of scanning speed, which was attributed to the hierarchical porous structure, reasonable pore distribution and good electrical conductivity in HSDCM-3. It also had a proportion of pseudocapacitance, which was due to the modification of CQDs, the conductivity and heteroatomic content of the material increased, and the contribution ratio of pseudocapacitance was increased.

Nyquist curves of series materials were shown in Fig. 6g. The equivalent series resistance (Rs), charge transfer resistance (Rct), and diffusion impedance (Zw, calculated by Eqs. 4–6) [46] were listed in Table. S1. It could be seen that the Rct value (0.125 Ω) of HSDCM-3 was small, indicating that it had a small load transfer resistance. The Rs and Zw of HSDCM-3 were 0.607 Ω and 0.368 Ω, respectively, and the mass and load transfer resistance in all samples were relatively small (Table. S1). The average electronic conductivity of the material measured by Four Point Probe Resistivity Measurements was 4.10 × 104 S/cm (Table. S2). This was due to the large SSA, hierarchical porous structure, and a certain degree of graphitization. It was worth noting that the Rs of the optimal sample HSCM-3 was 0.690 Ω, while that of HSDCM-3 in the present chapter was 0.607 Ω. In other words, under the condition of using the same amount of activator, the internal resistance of HSDCM-3 became smaller, which was due to the improvement of the conductivity of the overall material by CQDs composite modification. As shown in Fig. 6h, the phase angle of HSDCM-3 was − 76°, it was near the ideal electronic double layer capacitance characteristic (−90°). The relaxation times of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3, HSDCM-4 and HSCM-3 at − 45° were calculated by τ
0 = 1/ f 0 equation to be 3.45, 1.89, 2.78 and 1.92 s, respectively. It could be found that the τ
0 of HSDCM-3 was slightly smaller than that of HSCM-3, which indicated that both samples had better ion transport and diffusion capacity and that high graphitization degree and abundant pore structure were conducive to rapid ion diffusion and transport. In Fig. 6i, the calculated Warbug factors δ of HSDCM-2, HSDCM-3, HSDCM-4 and HSCM-3 were 9.7, 13.5, 8.4 and 14.4, respectively. The Warburg factor was inversely proportional to the diffusion coefficient [47], so the diffusion constants of HSDCM-4 and HSDCM-2 were both greater than that of HSDCM-3, which might be caused that the lower activation increased the conductivity of the material and the higher activation caused the increase of the defects of the material and accelerated the diffusion rate. The diffusion coefficient of HSDCM-3 was slightly higher than that of HSCM-3, which was due to the improvement of the overall electrical conductivity of the material by CQDs modification. Therefore, the rate of ion diffusion in the material was increased. HSDCM-3 had a relatively small diffusion constant and fast relaxation time, which were one of the reasons for its optimal capacitance performance.

In addition, we also assembled a symmetric supercapacitor using DES as electrolyte and HSDCM-3 as electrode material. Fig. 7a showed the CV curves of different sweep speeds (10–50 mV s−1) at 2 V, and there was no obvious deformation, indicating the dominant EDLC. Fig. 7b showed the current density from 1 to 5 A g−1, the calculated specific capacitance was 82, 62.1, 50.9, 41.27 and 34.2 F g−1, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7c, the energy densities could reach 45.6 Wh kg−1 and 19.0 Wh kg−1 respectively at 500 W kg−1 and 2.5 kW kg−1 power densities. Compared with our previous work [26] and other reported results (Table. S3), HSDCM-3 electrode had higher energy density and better rate performance. In addition, the capacitive performance of the device at different temperatures was also tested. The maximum energy densities at − 25, 0, 25, 50, 75 and 105 ℃ could reach up to 17.5, 23.5, 45.6, 30.6, 26.9 and 20.6 Wh kg−1, respectively, indicating that the device had a wide operating temperature range. The cycle performance tested at the current density of 10 A g −1 was shown in Fig. 7d, the performance of the materials decreased after 2500 cycles, it might be due to the pseudocapacitance provided by the heteroatoms decreased, leading to the decrease of the capacitor performance, with the increase of cycling times, active substances produced for self-activation effect, and reachable specific surface area increased [48], so the performance of the capacitors was increased, and after 20,000 cycles the device achieved a capacitance retention of 84.2%, showing excellent cycle stability.

In this work, the CQDs decorated N, S, O self-doped porous carbon material was prepared by hydrothermal pre-carbonization followed activation process. The addition of H2SO4 in the hydrothermal process could increase the degree of pre-carbonization of CM, and it was also beneficial to the porosity in CM. CQDs adsorbed to the surface and the channel of the pore, after activated by K2CO3, it got a high heteroatom doping amount, large SSA, hierarchical porous structure and high graphitization degree of carbon material. It exhibited excellent specific capacitance (477 F g−1 in 6 M KOH). The assembled symmetrical supercapacitor using the prepared porous carbon as electrode and K2CO3-glycol-arginine DES as electrolyte exhibited a remarkable energy density of 45.6 Wh kg−1. This work provided a novel method for the preparation of high capacitive performance carbon quantum dots decorated porous carbon electrode materials.
Tao Wang: Data Processing and Writing. Lina Peng: Revising. Dongling Wu: Supervision and Reviewing. Bolang Chen: Data Processing. Bin Jia: Data Acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22269022), and Natural Science Foundation of Xinjiang Autonomous Region (No. 2022D01C24).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2023.169499.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
